items.question_text: What should be recorded when handling flight plan information?

choices.text: The flight plan information required by the type of flight plan and the existing circumstances.
Only the aircraft identification and destination, unless the pilot asks for more detail.
Only the items needed for the active control position, leaving the rest to the next facility.
Only the items that do not already appear in automation.

items.explanation: The paragraph ties recording to the flight plan type and the actual operating circumstances.

items.question_text: Use authorized abbreviations when possible when recording flight plan information.
items.explanation: Authorized abbreviations are used whenever possible during flight plan recording.

items.question_text: When flight plans are filed directly with the center, en route controllers must record all items given by the pilot either on a flight progress strip or flight data entry, or on a voice recorder.
items.explanation: Direct center filing requires all pilot-given items to be recorded, either on strip/data entry or on a voice recorder.

items.question_text: If the en route controller records the pilot's flight plan information on a voice recorder, the controller must enter the sector or position number in box 26 of the initial flight progress strip.
items.explanation: Box 26 identifies where the recorded information can be found if SAR activity later becomes necessary.

items.situation: A pilot files directly with the center. The controller records the details on a voice recorder instead of a strip entry, then leaves box 26 blank because the information is already preserved somewhere in the audio system.
items.para_context: When a flight plan is filed directly with the center and the information is recorded on a voice recorder, box 26 of the initial flight progress strip identifies the sector or position number so the information can be found if SAR activity becomes necessary.

choices.text: Enter the sector or position number in box 26 so the recorded information can be located later if necessary.
Leave box 26 blank because the voice recorder itself replaces any strip annotation.
Rewrite the flight plan from memory after the aircraft departs instead of marking the strip now.
Record only the destination and beacon code because SAR can request the rest from the pilot later.

items.explanation: The strip still needs a locator to the recorded information when the voice recorder is used.

items.question_text: Outside EAS FDP automatic forwarding, what should be done with flight plan information?

choices.text: Forward it to the appropriate ATC facility, FSS, or BASOPS and record the time of filing and delivery on the form.
Keep it locally until the next facility requests it by interphone.
Forward it only to ATC facilities and not to FSS or BASOPS.
Record only the time of filing, since delivery time will always be automatic.

items.explanation: Manual forwarding includes both the transmission target and the requirement to record filing and delivery times.

items.question_text: Except during EAS FDP operation, controllers must forward flight plan information to the appropriate ATC facility, FSS, or BASOPS and record the time of filing and delivery on the form.
items.explanation: That is the baseline forwarding rule when EAS FDP automation is not handling the exchange.

items.question_text: If EAS FDP is operating but the data is not forwarded automatically by the computer, en route controllers must perform the manual forwarding actions.
items.explanation: EAS FDP removes the manual step only when the computer actually forwards the data automatically.

items.question_text: The forwarding responsibility for proposed tower en route flight plans and related amendments belongs to the appropriate departure terminal facility.
items.explanation: That forwarding target applies specifically to proposed tower en route flight plans and their amendments.

items.situation: EAS FDP is operating, but a flight plan does not forward automatically. Another controller says no manual action is needed because the system is in EAS FDP mode, and a proposed tower en route amendment can wait for the next facility to pull it later.
items.para_context: During EAS FDP operation, manual forwarding is still required when the computer does not forward the data automatically. Proposed tower en route flight plans and related amendments are forwarded to the appropriate departure terminal facility.

choices.text: Perform the manual forwarding actions and forward the proposed tower en route amendment to the appropriate departure terminal facility.
Wait for automation to retry the transmission and do nothing manually while EAS FDP is active.
Forward the amendment only after the aircraft departs, since departure terminal facilities do not need proposed changes.
Record the filing time only and skip delivery documentation because the system mode is automatic.

items.explanation: EAS FDP does not remove manual responsibility when the computer fails to forward the data.

items.question_text: When must terminal controllers forward aircraft departure times for VFR flights?

choices.text: When FSSs or military operations offices have requested them.
For every VFR departure, regardless of whether anyone requested the times.
Only when the pilot requests departure times be forwarded.
Only when the departure is from a joint-use airport.

items.explanation: Departure-time forwarding depends on the FSS or military operations office having requested those times.

items.question_text: Forward aircraft departure times to FSSs or military operations offices when they have requested them.
items.explanation: The terminal rule directs forwarding of departure times when the receiving office has requested them.

items.question_text: If a pilot has not requested other VFR flight plan data to be forwarded, controllers should not forward that other VFR data.
items.explanation: Other VFR flight plan data is forwarded only when the pilot requests it.

items.question_text: This forwarding rule applies to terminal handling of aircraft departure times and other VFR flight plan data.
items.explanation: The paragraph is a terminal-only forwarding rule for VFR departure times and other VFR data.

items.situation: An FSS has requested VFR departure times from your tower. A pilot also departs on a VFR flight plan but has not asked for any additional VFR data to be forwarded. Another controller wants to forward the full VFR plan to be safe.
items.para_context: In terminal operations, forward aircraft departure times to FSSs or military operations offices when they have requested them. Forward other VFR flight plan data only if requested by the pilot.

choices.text: Forward the departure time because it was requested, but do not forward the other VFR flight plan data unless the pilot asks for it.
Forward the entire VFR flight plan automatically any time an FSS has requested departure times.
Do not forward anything unless both the receiving office and the pilot separately request the same information.
Forward the other VFR data first and wait to send the departure time until after the pilot lands.

items.explanation: The departure-time request and the pilot-request condition are separate triggers.

items.question_text: What must terminal controllers forward on all DVFR departures from joint-use airports?

choices.text: Departure times to the military operations office.
The full flight plan to the FSS only.
Only the destination and requested altitude.
Nothing unless the pilot asks for the data to be forwarded.

items.explanation: All DVFR departures from joint-use airports require the departure time to be forwarded to the military operations office.

items.question_text: Terminal controllers must forward departure times on all DVFR departures from joint-use airports to the military operations office.
items.explanation: The word all is part of the rule: every DVFR departure from a joint-use airport gets the departure time forwarded to military operations.

items.question_text: If a civil pilot is departing DVFR from a joint-use airport, the pilot should include the phrase DVFR to destination in the initial call-up to an FAA-operated tower.
items.explanation: The note gives that initial-call phrase specifically for civil pilots departing DVFR from a joint-use airport.

items.situation: A civil pilot is departing DVFR from a joint-use airport for Little Rock and is making the initial call to an FAA-operated tower. Build the required DVFR portion of the call.

A DVFR departure leaves a joint-use airport. Another controller says the departure time does not need to be forwarded because the flight is civil rather than military, and the pilot's initial call omitted the words DVFR to destination.
items.para_context: All DVFR departures from joint-use airports require forwarding the departure time to the military operations office. Civil pilots departing DVFR from a joint-use airport include the phrase DVFR to destination in the initial call-up to an FAA-operated tower.

choices.text: Forward the departure time to military operations and recognize that civil DVFR departures from joint-use airports are still supposed to identify themselves as DVFR to destination on initial call-up.
Skip the forwarding requirement because only military DVFR departures from joint-use airports are covered.
Treat the missing DVFR phrase as eliminating the need to forward any departure data.
Forward the data only if the pilot restates the full destination on a second initial call-up.

items.explanation: The forwarding rule applies to all DVFR departures from joint-use airports, and the note separately describes the civil pilot's expected DVFR phrase.

items.question_text: What should ATC ask a pilot to do when the pilot wants to change from an IFR to a VFR flight plan?

choices.text: Cancel the IFR flight plan directly with the center controller on frequency.
Wait until landing and then close the flight plan with the tower.
Advise the next ATC facility and let that facility handle the plan change automatically.

items.explanation: The controller's action is to request the pilot contact the appropriate FSS.

items.question_text: Request the pilot to contact the appropriate FSS to change from an IFR to a VFR flight plan.
items.explanation: That imperative is the whole rule of the paragraph.

items.question_text: If a pilot informs ATC of a desire to change from an IFR to a VFR flight plan, controllers must request that the pilot contact the appropriate FSS.
items.explanation: The controller does not make the flight plan change directly; the pilot is directed to the appropriate FSS.

items.question_text: This request applies when a pilot informs ATC of a desire to change from an IFR to a VFR flight plan.
items.explanation: The paragraph is narrowly scoped to a pilot-initiated request to change from IFR to VFR.

items.situation: A pilot reports that the weather ahead is good and says, "I'd like to change this from IFR to VFR right now." Another controller responds that you can just make the change locally without involving anyone else because the pilot is already on your frequency.
items.para_context: When a pilot informs ATC of a desire to change from an IFR to a VFR flight plan, the controller requests that the pilot contact the appropriate FSS.

choices.text: Request the pilot to contact the appropriate FSS.
Change the flight plan locally on frequency and tell the pilot the FSS will see it later.
Tell the pilot to wait until landing to make any flight plan change.
Direct the pilot to contact only the next ATC facility instead of the FSS.

items.explanation: The controller's prescribed action is to send the pilot to the appropriate FSS for the IFR-to-VFR plan change.

items.question_text: When handling a flight plan, what determines which information must be recorded?
items.explanation: Recording depends on the type of flight plan and the existing circumstances.

choices.text: The type of flight plan and the existing circumstances.
Only the pilot's initial route request.
Only the destination airport and beacon code.
Only the items that automation does not already display.

items.question_text: What should be used when possible while recording flight plan information?
items.explanation: The paragraph directs the use of authorized abbreviations when possible.

items.question_text: When flight plans are filed directly with the center, where may en route controllers record all items given by the pilot?
items.explanation: The controller may record them on a flight progress strip or data entry, or on a voice recorder.

choices.text: On a flight progress strip or flight data entry, or on a voice recorder.
Only on a paper strip and never on a recorder.
Only in box 26 of the initial strip.

items.question_text: If the flight plan information is kept on a voice recorder, what is entered in box 26 of the initial flight progress strip?
items.explanation: Box 26 gets the sector or position number so the recorded information can be found later if needed.

items.question_text: Authorized abbreviations should be used when possible while recording flight plan information.
items.explanation: That directive appears in the opening sentence of the paragraph.

items.question_text: Except during EAS FDP operation, where is flight plan information forwarded?
items.explanation: The information goes to the appropriate ATC facility, FSS, or BASOPS.

choices.text: To the appropriate ATC facility, FSS, or BASOPS.
Only to the next ATC facility in line.
Only to BASOPS, regardless of flight type.
Only to the departure terminal facility.

items.question_text: What times are recorded on the form when flight plan information is forwarded manually?
items.explanation: The form records the time of filing and the time of delivery.

choices.text: The time of filing and the time of delivery.
The time of departure and the estimated time en route.

items.question_text: During EAS FDP operation, when are the manual forwarding actions still required?
items.explanation: The manual actions are still required when the data is not forwarded automatically by the computer.

choices.text: When the data is not forwarded automatically by the computer.
Whenever a controller prefers manual backup, even if the computer forwarded it.
Only when the pilot asks whether the flight plan was sent.

items.question_text: What facility receives proposed tower en route flight plans and related amendments?
items.explanation: Those items go to the appropriate departure terminal facility.

choices.text: The next center sector by default.

items.question_text: During EAS FDP operation, interfaced automated facilities record both the forwarded data and the time of transmission automatically.
items.explanation: That is the note's description of normal automated exchange during EAS FDP.

items.question_text: When should terminal controllers forward VFR departure times to an FSS or military operations office?
items.explanation: Departure times are forwarded when the FSS or military operations office has requested them.

choices.text: When that office has requested the departure times.
Only when the pilot asks for forwarding.

items.question_text: Who must request the forwarding of other VFR flight plan data before it is forwarded?
items.explanation: Other VFR flight plan data is forwarded only if requested by the pilot.

items.question_text: This forwarding rule applies to terminal handling of VFR data.
items.explanation: The paragraph is explicitly labeled terminal.

items.question_text: If the pilot has not requested other VFR flight plan data to be forwarded, what should controllers do with that other VFR data?
items.explanation: It is not forwarded unless the pilot asks for it.

choices.text: Forward it automatically along with the departure time.
Forward it only to military operations.
Hold it until the arrival facility asks for it.

items.question_text: Other VFR flight plan data is forwarded only if requested by the _____
items.explanation: The trigger is a pilot request.

items.question_text: What must be forwarded on all DVFR departures from joint-use airports?
items.explanation: The required forwarded item is the departure time.

choices.text: Nothing unless the pilot asks for data forwarding.

items.question_text: To whom are departure times forwarded for all DVFR departures from joint-use airports?
items.explanation: They go to the military operations office.

items.question_text: What phrase should a civil pilot departing DVFR from a joint-use airport include in the initial call-up to an FAA-operated tower?
items.explanation: The note gives the phrase DVFR to destination.

items.question_text: All DVFR departures from joint-use airports require departure times to be forwarded to the military operations office.
items.explanation: The paragraph states the rule with all, not just some categories of DVFR flights.

items.question_text: Complete the DVFR initial-call phrase: "DVFR TO _____."
items.explanation: The note uses destination as the variable part of the phrase.

items.question_text: If a pilot wants to change from an IFR to a VFR flight plan, who should the pilot be told to contact?
items.explanation: The pilot is directed to the appropriate FSS.

choices.text: The next ARTCC sector on the route.

items.question_text: What should a controller request when a pilot informs ATC of a desire to change from an IFR to a VFR flight plan?
items.explanation: The controller asks the pilot to contact the appropriate FSS.

choices.text: Request the pilot to contact the appropriate FSS.
Change the flight plan locally and advise the next facility later.
Tell the pilot to close the plan only after landing.
Keep the pilot on IFR until BASOPS clears the change.

items.question_text: This request applies when the pilot informs ATC of a desire to change from an IFR to a VFR flight plan.
items.explanation: That is the scope of the rule.

items.question_text: ATC should make the IFR-to-VFR flight plan change directly on frequency without involving FSS if the pilot is already on the controller's frequency.
items.explanation: The rule does not authorize ATC to handle the change directly that way; the pilot is sent to the appropriate FSS.

items.question_text: Complete the referral target for an IFR-to-VFR flight plan change: "appropriate _____."
items.explanation: The referral target is the appropriate FSS.
